input.instructions:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-S4-ahc030-polyomino-mining
Title: S4. AHC030 / Polyomino Mining

Problem:
Given known polyomino oil-field shapes but unknown placements, recover all oil-containing cells with as little probing cost as possible using drilling, noisy aggregate queries, and final guesses.

Grading target:
Lower probing cost is better.
Contest score is derived from absolute and relative cost.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

variables.selected_prompt_text:
Write a tentative answer and ask one reviewer child to perform a rigorous technical critique. The reviewer should specifically look for logical gaps, edge cases, or inefficiencies in the proposed solution. Ensure the reviewer attempts to validate the correctness of the reasoning before the final response is produced.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "review",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "draft_response": "plain-text draft answer",
  "message_for_reviewer": "plain-text review request for the child"
}
variables.child_selected_prompt_text:
Solve the question directly.
Keep the entire JSON compact.
Keep decision_notes extremely short. Prefer 3 to 8 words.
Do not narrate your reasoning outside the response field.
In the response field, use exactly two short lines encoded with \n:
FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>
Keep the justification to 12 words or fewer.
Use plain text only inside the response field. Do not use markdown formatting, code fences, or math-style backslashes such as \equiv.
Do not add any extra text before or after the JSON object.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "execute_locally",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "response": "FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>\nJUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>",
  "result_type": "summary"
}

orchestration.decision_notes: The user is asking for a solution to a specific competitive programming problem (AHC030). Based on the child result, the previous attempt was deemed too high-level. I will provide a structured algorithmic approach that addresses the core optimization problem: minimizing probing cost while maximizing information gain.

Question:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-S4-ahc030-polyomino-mining
Title: S4. AHC030 / Polyomino Mining

Problem:
Given known polyomino oil-field shapes but unknown placements, recover all oil-containing cells with as little probing cost as possible using drilling, noisy aggregate queries, and final guesses.

Grading target:
Lower probing cost is better.
Contest score is derived from absolute and relative cost.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

results.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To solve the Polyomino Mining problem with minimal probing cost, implement a Bayesian-inspired adaptive sampling strategy combined with a geometric pattern-matching algorithm. 1. Probabilistic Mapping: Maintain a probability map P(c) for every cell c in the grid, representing the likelihood that c belongs to an oil-containing polyomino. Initially, P(c) is determined by the density and possible placements of the known polyomino shapes. 2. Information-Theoretic Probing (Active Learning): Instead of random drilling, select probe locations x that maximize the Expected Information Gain (EIG). Since queries are noisy aggregates, use a Bayesian update rule: P(c | query) proportional to P(query | c) * P(c). The objective function for selecting the next probe is to minimize the entropy of the global probability distribution. 3. Geometric Constraint Satisfaction: Use the known polyomino shapes as rigid templates. When a probe returns a signal, use a RANSAC-style or template-matching approach to filter possible polyomino placements. This significantly prunes the search space compared to cell-by-cell analysis. 4. Drilling vs. Aggregate Strategy: - Use Aggregate Queries early in the process to identify hot zones (high-density areas) across large regions. - Use Drilling (point queries) only when the uncertainty in a specific high-probability cluster exceeds a threshold, or to confirm the exact boundary of a detected polyomino. 5. Final Guessing: Once the cost budget is nearly exhausted, perform a Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation to select the set of cells that maximizes the likelihood of containing the polyominoes given all collected noisy data. JUSTIFICATION: This approach minimizes cost by prioritizing high-entropy regions through aggregate queries and uses the geometric rigidity of polyominoes to compensate for query noise, preventing redundant drilling.
